review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Authentication completion is duplicated across web entrypoints instead of living behind one application-service boundary.

evidence: `AuthController.verifyGoogleToken()` normalizes claims, upserts the user, and mints a JWT inline; `verifyMicrosoftToken()` repeats the same completion workflow for a different provider.

`OAuth2SuccessHandler.onAuthenticationSuccess()` repeats the normalized-identity -> `userService.findOrCreateUser()` -> `jwtTokenService.generateToken()` -> `authCodeStore.storeJwt()` sequence for server-side logins.

`AuthController` also owns Microsoft challenge-session cookie handling and auth-code exchange, making one controller the coordination point for several auth sub-protocols rather than a thin transport adapter.
detail.suggestion: Extract a provider-neutral authentication application service that owns normalized-identity completion, JWT minting, and auth-code issuance, then keep controllers and success handlers focused on HTTP/OAuth protocol translation only.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: The documented runtime provider-config boundary has drifted into a backend/frontend-specific loose shape.

evidence: The design document requires `/api/auth/providers` to return a stable schema where disabled Microsoft config is still represented explicitly with `null`/empty values.
`ProviderConfigController.getProviders()` only adds Microsoft `clientId` and `authority` when Microsoft client-side login is enabled, so disabled and enabled responses have different shapes.

`normalizeProviderConfig()` accepts partial objects and backfills defaults on the frontend, which means the consumer is compensating for a drifting boundary instead of enforcing one published contract.
detail.suggestion: Replace the stringly `Map<String, Object>` provider payload with a typed backend DTO that always emits the full schema, and have the frontend consume that exact contract without partial-object normalization.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: Provider ownership is scattered across framework layers instead of a clear feature slice

evidence: The backend's top-level packages under `com.demo.sso` are generic technical layers (`config`, `controller`, `model`, `repository`, `service`) rather than feature areas.

Implementing or changing Microsoft auth spans `MicrosoftAuthProperties`, `MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter`, `AuthController`, `ProviderConfigController`, `MicrosoftTokenVerifier`, and `RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore`, which distributes one provider's behavior across multiple unrelated package owners.

On the frontend, the same provider is split between `HomePage` (composition), `MicrosoftClientSideLogin` (flow), `useProviderConfig` (contract fetch), and `types/auth.ts` (public contract), so ownership is similarly reconstructed by following references instead of opening one provider-focused module.
detail.suggestion: Reorganize auth code around explicit provider-focused areas—at minimum co-locate Microsoft and Google flow wiring with their public contracts, keeping only the shared identity/JWT core in common modules—so a provider change is navigated through one slice instead of every framework layer.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: The shared frontend API client discards backend error messages and throws status-only errors.

evidence: `frontend/src/api/client.ts` throws `new Error(\`API error: ${response.status}\`)` for every non-OK response and never reads the response body.

`AuthController` returns specific `{error: ...}` bodies such as `Invalid Google credential`, `Invalid Microsoft credential`, `Invalid or expired code`, and `Microsoft client-side login is disabled`, but that detail is lost before any frontend caller can inspect it.
detail.suggestion: Update `apiFetch` to parse JSON/text error bodies on non-2xx responses and throw a structured error that preserves both HTTP status and the backend `error` message, then let auth callers use that preserved detail instead of status-only failures.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: The homepage silently swallows auth-code exchange failures at the login boundary.

evidence: `HomePage` exchanges `?code=` through `apiFetch('/auth/exchange')` and then uses `.catch(() => { /* Code was invalid or expired — stay on homepage */ })` with no log, no telemetry, and no visible error state.
That means an expired code and a network failure both collapse into the same 'nothing happened' outcome, making the failure mode impossible to distinguish during debugging or normal use.
detail.suggestion: Add local error state in `HomePage` and surface a visible message when `/auth/exchange` fails, at minimum distinguishing invalid/expired codes from transport failures instead of swallowing both silently.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Google and Microsoft client-side login cards expose failures with different strategies.

evidence: `ClientSideLogin.handleSuccess` catches `/auth/google/verify` failures and only calls `console.error('Login failed:', error)`.

`MicrosoftClientSideLogin` catches its login/verify failures and sets a user-visible `error` message, so sibling client-side providers on the same screen teach developers different expectations about how auth errors surface.
detail.suggestion: Give `ClientSideLogin` the same explicit UI error state pattern used by `MicrosoftClientSideLogin` (or move both cards to a shared auth-error presentation helper) so sibling flows report failures consistently.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Invalid legacy login-method values are silently converted to null instead of treated as errors.

evidence: `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod` returns null for blank input and also catches `IllegalArgumentException` for invalid values, returning null again.

`UserService.findOrCreateUser(String...)` passes that result into `NormalizedIdentity.google(...)`, and `applyProviderIdentityFields` simply skips `lastLoginFlow` / `loginMethod` when the flow is null, so malformed legacy input is accepted without any signal while the rest of the auth pipeline uses exceptions for invalid identity data.
detail.suggestion: Make `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod` reject unrecognized nonblank values with `IllegalArgumentException` (or return `Optional<AuthFlow>` and force `UserService` to handle invalidity explicitly) so bad legacy login-method values do not disappear silently.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::leg.summary: Google-specific names still label provider-neutral identity data across core auth APIs

evidence: `UserService.legacyGoogleCompatibilityId(...)` writes non-Google identities into `User.googleId`, so the field name no longer matches the value it carries.

`UserRepository.findByGoogleId(...)` and `JwtTokenService.generateToken(String email, String googleId)` extend that Google-only term into repository and JWT APIs that now participate in a multi-provider identity model.
`AuthenticatedUserIdentity` still exposes `legacyGoogleId`, even though that path represents the old contract shape rather than a reliably Google-only concept.
detail.suggestion: Rename the compatibility-facing identifiers to provider-neutral terms such as `legacyIdentityKey` or `legacyProviderUserId` across the entity/service/JWT/repository APIs, and keep any truly Google-specific naming only at explicit backward-compatibility boundaries like the persisted column or legacy token claim.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::log.summary: `loginMethod` is named like an auth method but stores client/server flow values

evidence: `UserService.applyProviderIdentityFields(...)` stores `identity.loginFlow().name()` via `user.setLoginMethod(...)`, so values like `SERVER_SIDE` and `CLIENT_SIDE` are written into a field called method.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: CSRF exclusion uses /auth/microsoft/* (single-segment) while permitAll uses /auth/** (multi-segment)

evidence: In SecurityConfig.filterChain(), CSRF is disabled via ignoringRequestMatchers("/auth/google/verify", "/auth/microsoft/*", "/auth/exchange", "/auth/logout"). The /auth/microsoft/* pattern only matches one path segment (e.g., /auth/microsoft/verify, /auth/microsoft/challenge) while the authorization rule uses /auth/** which matches all depths. This is functionally correct today since Microsoft endpoints are exactly one level deep, but the inconsistent wildcard style (/microsoft/* vs /**) could lead to a gap if a nested endpoint is added.
detail.suggestion: Either enumerate Microsoft endpoints explicitly (matching the Google and Exchange style) for maximum clarity, or use /auth/microsoft/** for consistency with the permitAll pattern. Explicit enumeration is safer: ignoringRequestMatchers("/auth/google/verify", "/auth/microsoft/challenge", "/auth/microsoft/verify", "/auth/exchange", "/auth/logout").

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: POST /auth/logout returns success but performs no server-side JWT invalidation or token blocklisting

evidence: AuthController.logout() simply returns ResponseEntity.ok(new LogoutResponse("Logged out successfully")) with no server-side action — no token blocklist check, no Redis expiry, no SecurityContext clearing. The frontend removes the JWT from localStorage, but the token remains cryptographically valid until expiry (86400000ms = 24 hours). A stolen token continues to work even after the user 'logs out'.
detail.suggestion: If full revocation is not needed, add a brief Javadoc comment on the logout method documenting the design decision (stateless JWT, client-side only, no revocation). If revocation is desired, implement a Redis-backed token blocklist and check it in JwtAuthenticationFilter.validateAndExtract(). The documentation approach is the simpler and likely appropriate path for this experimental project.

detail.suggestion: Inject a resolved key-prefix strategy (e.g., a record or enum value) at construction time so the store doesn't query rollout properties per-call. The migration mode should be a construction-time parameter, not a runtime query.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::res.summary: resolveDisplayName() resolves email identifiers but is named as if it resolves display names

evidence: Line 40: `String email = resolveDisplayName(claims.email(), claims.preferredUsername(), claims.upn())` — the result is assigned to variable `email`, showing the caller expects an email identifier, not a display name
Line 60: `private static String resolveDisplayName(String... candidates)` picks the first non-blank value from Microsoft's email-like claim priority cascade (email > preferred_username > upn)

The method was renamed from the previously-flagged `firstNonBlank` (honestly generic) to `resolveDisplayName` (actively misleading about what is being resolved) — the fix for the original issue introduced a different naming problem
detail.suggestion: Rename to `resolveEmailIdentifier` or `selectPrimaryEmailClaim` to communicate that this implements Microsoft's email claim priority cascade. The method picks the best available email-like claim, not a display name.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Google identity reaches ProviderIdentityNormalizer via two different shapes depending on auth flow

evidence: In OAuth2SuccessHandler.normalizeIdentity() (line 82-88), Google identity is reconstructed from raw OAuth2User.getAttribute() calls into a VerifiedGoogleIdentity record. In AuthController.verifyGoogleToken() (line 87), GoogleTokenVerifier.verify() returns a typed VerifiedGoogleIdentity directly. Both paths then feed into ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeGoogleClaims() — same destination, two different assembly shapes upstream.
detail.suggestion: Extract Google identity claim extraction from OAuth2User into GoogleTokenVerifier (or a shared GoogleIdentityExtractor) so both server-side and client-side flows produce VerifiedGoogleIdentity at the same point. review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: AuthenticatedUserIdentity dual-nullable design forces runtime isLegacy() branching at user-lookup boundary

evidence: AuthenticatedUserIdentity has userId nullable (legacy) and provider/providerUserId nullable (legacy), checked via isLegacy() (contractVersion == null). UserService.findCurrentUser() branches on isLegacy() to choose findByEmail vs findById. UserController.getCurrentUser() also branches on instanceof to detect legacy principal type. Two separate files must duplicate the understanding of which identity variant they received.

The legacy factory sets userId=null, provider=null, providerUserId=null, contractVersion=null. The v2 factory requires all four. Callers must remember which fields are safe to access based on isLegacy() — no compile-time enforcement.
detail.suggestion: Replace the single record with a sealed interface (e.g., sealed interface AuthenticatedUserIdentity permits LegacyIdentity, V2Identity) so each variant carries exactly its fields. UserService.findCurrentUser would pattern-match on the variant, eliminating nullable-field branching and making the boundary contract compile-time safe.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken mixes cookie extraction, nonce lifecycle, and token verification in one method

evidence: verifyMicrosoftToken (lines 118-156) performs five distinct responsibilities: (1) rollout property check, (2) verifier availability check, (3) request validation, (4) cookie-based session ID extraction via currentChallengeSessionId, (5) nonce consumption via microsoftChallengeStore.consumeNonce, and (6) token verification via microsoftTokenVerifier. The method orchestrates session management, challenge lifecycle, and identity verification as tangled sequential steps rather than composing distinct collaborators.
detail.suggestion: Extract challenge validation into a dedicated method or service (e.g., MicrosoftChallengeValidator.validateAndConsumeNonce(request, challengeId)) that encapsulates cookie extraction and nonce consumption. The controller method would then read as: validate request → validate challenge → verify token → complete authentication.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::no_f.summary: No test validates the combined filter chain ordering that is critical to auth enforcement

evidence: SecurityConfig.java (lines 67-68) positions MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter before OAuth2AuthorizationRequestRedirectFilter and JwtAuthenticationFilter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter. This ordering is security-critical — incorrect ordering would bypass the gate filter or JWT validation.

SecurityConfigTest.java tests endpoint access patterns (public vs protected, JSON error body, no JSESSIONID) but does not inspect the filter chain to verify that the custom filters are positioned correctly relative to framework filters.
JwtAuthenticationFilterTest tests the filter logic in isolation with mocked FilterChain but does not verify it runs before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter in the real chain.
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilterTest similarly tests gate logic in isolation without verifying chain position.
detail.suggestion: Add an integration test that injects the SecurityFilterChain bean, iterates its filters, and asserts that MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter appears before OAuth2AuthorizationRequestRedirectFilter and JwtAuthenticationFilter appears before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter. Alternatively, add a behavioral test that sends a request to a gated Microsoft endpoint and verifies the gate filter fires before the OAuth2 redirect filter.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::no_c.summary: No shared contract test validates frontend API client expectations against backend controller responses

evidence: Frontend apiFetch expects responses with specific shapes: {token: string} from /auth/exchange, {challengeId, nonce} from /auth/microsoft/challenge, UserProfile shape from /user/me. Backend DTOs define these shapes in Java records.

Frontend E2E tests mock API responses in page.route() handlers with hand-constructed JSON, so they validate the frontend's ability to consume shapes but not that the backend actually produces those shapes.
If a backend DTO field is renamed (e.g., UserResponse.pictureUrl to imageUrl), no test would catch the frontend/backend contract mismatch until E2E tests run against a real backend.
detail.suggestion: Add a lightweight contract test or shared type validation: either (1) a backend test that serializes DTOs and asserts field names match frontend expectations, or (2) use the backend's OpenAPI spec (if generated) to validate frontend types, or (3) add at least one cross-stack E2E test that hits the real backend (the api-health.spec.ts pattern exists but is conditional on PLAYWRIGHT_API_BASE_URL).

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: SecurityConfig authentication entry point writes raw Map JSON instead of serializing ErrorResponse DTO

LogoutResponse serializes to {"message": "..."} — plain record field, no annotation

detail.suggestion: Consider splitting into config/properties/, config/filter/ (or security/filter/), and keeping SecurityConfig at config/ root. This would make each sub-package single-responsibility. At 6 files this is not urgent but becomes important as the auth system grows.

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: Both model/ and service/model/ exist, creating naming ambiguity about where domain objects vs service-layer value objects belong

evidence: model/ contains JPA entities (User) and enums (AuthProvider, AuthFlow)
service/model/ contains service-layer value objects (AuthenticatedUserIdentity implements Principal, NormalizedIdentity is an intermediate auth representation)
A developer unfamiliar with the codebase cannot tell from directory names alone which 'model' package to use for new classes
detail.suggestion: Rename service/model/ to service/identity/ or service/dto/ to better communicate that these are authentication workflow artifacts, not persistence entities. This makes the naming self-documenting.

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: service/user/ contains only UserService.java — a single-file package adds directory depth without organizational benefit

Other service sub-packages (auth/3, challenge/4, token/4) have multiple files justifying their existence
detail.suggestion: Either move UserService.java directly under service/ or plan for growth (e.g., adding UserProfileService, UserMigrationService). If the package will remain a singleton, flattening reduces navigation overhead.

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: RedisAuthCodeStore in service/challenge/ directly imports config.AuthRolloutProperties for feature flag checking, creating a coupling-direction concern

Service implementations should receive configuration values through constructor injection rather than depending on the config package directly
JwtTokenService also imports AuthRolloutProperties, suggesting this is a pattern rather than a one-off
detail.suggestion: This is a common Spring pattern (injecting @ConfigurationProperties beans) and is not strictly wrong, but if config/ grows into a complex module, consider introducing a simple interface or passing primitive config values to decouple service implementations from the config package structure.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: GoogleTokenVerifier eagerly creates NetHttpTransport and verifier builder at bean construction, regardless of whether Google auth is feature-flagged on

evidence: Lines 25-30: constructor calls new NetHttpTransport(), GsonFactory.getDefaultInstance(), and .build() at bean init time. The verifier object is fully constructed before any request arrives.
detail.suggestion: Consider @Lazy annotation on the bean or moving verifier construction into a lazy-init supplier, so that transport and verifier are only created when Google token verification is actually needed. Alternatively, gate the bean creation on google auth being enabled via @ConditionalOnProperty.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: DEFAULT_PROVIDER_CONFIG is computed at module load from import.meta.env.VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID — stale snapshot pattern

evidence: auth.ts:7 reads fallbackGoogleClientId = import.meta.env.VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID at module scope. Lines 45-56 compute DEFAULT_PROVIDER_CONFIG from this value. useProviderConfig.ts:10 uses DEFAULT_PROVIDER_CONFIG as initial state and line 29 uses it as error fallback. detail.suggestion: Move the error parameter extraction and history.replaceState() into a useEffect hook to separate state initialization from browser side effects. This aligns with React's rendering model and avoids double-execution under StrictMode.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: UserResponse.from() returns null for lastLoginFlow but empty string for all other nullable fields

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: JwtTokenService.generateToken has two consecutive Javadoc blocks — the first is orphaned dead documentation

evidence: Lines 85-92 contain a Javadoc block ('Mints a JWT for the given user, dispatching to legacy or V2 format...') immediately followed by lines 93-104 with another Javadoc block ('Generates a signed JWT for the given user...'). Java tooling only renders the last Javadoc before a declaration, so the first block with the migration note is silently orphaned and invisible in IDE hover or generated docs.

Merge the migration note from the first Javadoc block (lines 85-92) into the second block (lines 93-104) as a

Migration note: paragraph, then delete the orphaned block

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: ObjectProvider<MicrosoftTokenVerifier> implies optional bean but MicrosoftTokenVerifier is unconditionally registered

evidence: AuthController line 51 uses ObjectProvider<MicrosoftTokenVerifier>, and lines 126-129 check getIfAvailable() for null. But MicrosoftTokenVerifier is annotated @Service (line 10-11) with no @ConditionalOnProperty or @Profile annotation — it is always registered as a bean. The null check on line 127 is unreachable dead code, and the ObjectProvider contract misleads readers into thinking the verifier might not be available.
detail.suggestion: Either inject MicrosoftTokenVerifier directly (removing ObjectProvider and the dead null check) and rely solely on the rollout properties flag, or add @ConditionalOnProperty("app.microsoft.client-id") to MicrosoftTokenVerifier to make it genuinely optional — matching the ObjectProvider contract

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::reso.summary: resolveDisplayName is a generic coalesce-first-non-blank utility, not display-name-specific

evidence: Line 60: resolveDisplayName(String... candidates) returns the first non-blank string from a varargs list. It is called on line 40 to pick among email, preferredUsername, and upn — none of which are 'display names'. The method is a generic firstNonBlank coalesce.
detail.suggestion: Rename to firstNonBlank or coalesceNonBlank to reflect the generic utility purpose, making the call site at line 40 self-documenting

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::user.summary: UserResponse.from() null-coalesces fields that entity schema marks as NOT NULL

evidence: UserResponse.from() (lines 22-33) applies null-coalescing (e.g., user.getProvider() != null ? ... : '') for provider and providerUserId, but User.java marks these as @Column(nullable = false). The null checks are redundant for normally-persisted entities and suggest the code doesn't trust its own schema.
detail.suggestion: Remove null-coalescing for provider and providerUserId since they are schema-guaranteed non-null. Keep null-coalescing only for genuinely nullable fields (name, pictureUrl, lastLoginFlow). Add a brief comment noting which fields are nullable by design.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::authen.summary: AuthenticatedUserIdentity uses nullable fields instead of sealed hierarchy for legacy/V2 variants

evidence: Lines 14-20: The record uses nullable Long userId, nullable AuthProvider provider, nullable String providerUserId, and nullable Integer contractVersion to distinguish legacy from V2. The isLegacy() method on line 41 checks contractVersion == null. Callers must remember to check isLegacy() before accessing userId or provider — the type system does not prevent accessing null V2 fields on a legacy instance.
detail.suggestion: Replace with a sealed interface: sealed interface AuthenticatedUserIdentity permits LegacyIdentity, V2Identity. LegacyIdentity has non-null email and nullable googleId; V2Identity has non-null userId, email, provider, providerUserId. Pattern matching on the sealed type makes callers handle both variants exhaustively without nullable field access.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::micros.summary: MicrosoftIdTokenClaims makes all string fields nullable with no compile-time required/optional distinction

The fromMap factory on line 34 validates sub at runtime (line 49) but iss, tid, and other fields that are required by downstream consumers (ProviderIdentityNormalizer line 31 checks isBlank(claims.iss())) have no enforcement at construction time.
detail.suggestion: Add null/blank validation for iss and tid in fromMap() alongside the existing sub validation, or split the record into required fields (iss, sub, tid) and optional fields to make the contract explicit at the type level

review::.::holistic::type_safety::user_r.summary: UserResponse.from() converts AuthProvider and AuthFlow enums to raw strings, losing type safety at API boundary

evidence: Lines 28-29: user.getProvider().name() and user.getLastLoginFlow().name() convert enum values to String. The UserResponse record declares provider and lastLoginFlow as String (lines 14-17). While this is common for JSON serialization, it means the response type cannot be validated against the domain enum at compile time.
detail.suggestion: Declare provider as AuthProvider and lastLoginFlow as AuthFlow in UserResponse, relying on Jackson's enum serialization. This preserves type information and allows compile-time validation if the response is consumed internally.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::runtim.summary: JwtTokenService.generateV2Token runtime-checks for null on fields guaranteed non-null by JPA schema

But User.java declares email, provider, and providerUserId as @Column(nullable=false). The runtime check duplicates schema guarantees because the parameter type (User) doesn't encode the non-null invariant.
detail.suggestion: This is a defensive practice that's reasonable given that User can be constructed in memory without JPA enforcement. Consider adding a brief comment noting this is a defense against in-memory construction, or create a validated User value object for the V2 path.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::normal.summary: ProviderIdentityNormalizer relies entirely on runtime isBlank() checks for input validation

evidence: Lines 17, 31, 42: normalizeGoogleClaims and normalizeMicrosoftClaims use isBlank() runtime checks on fields from VerifiedGoogleIdentity and MicrosoftIdTokenClaims records. Since these records allow null/blank fields, the normalizer must do runtime validation that could be pushed into the record constructors or factory methods.
detail.suggestion: Push the required-field validation (sub must not be blank, email must not be blank) into the VerifiedGoogleIdentity and MicrosoftIdTokenClaims factory methods/constructors so that these records cannot exist with invalid required fields. This moves the check from runtime caller responsibility to type construction time.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::nullab.summary: AuthenticatedUserIdentity uses nullable fields to model two distinct variants (legacy vs v2) instead of a sealed type hierarchy

evidence: The record has nullable userId (null for legacy), nullable provider (null for legacy), nullable contractVersion (null for legacy), and nullable legacyProviderKey (null for v2). The isLegacy() method checks contractVersion == null to distinguish variants. This means a caller could accidentally access userId on a legacy identity and get null without compile-time warning.
detail.suggestion: Consider refactoring AuthenticatedUserIdentity into a sealed interface with two record implementations: LegacyIdentity(String email, String googleId) and V2Identity(Long userId, String email, AuthProvider provider, String providerUserId). This would make field availability type-safe at compile time. However, since this is a migration-era type that will simplify to V2-only eventually, the current approach with factory methods and runtime checks is pragmatically acceptable — the refactor may not be worth the churn if V2_ONLY deployment is imminent.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::apifet.summary: apiFetch<T> returns T | undefined, forcing all callers to handle undefined even when 204 responses are unexpected

detail.suggestion: Consider splitting into two functions: apiFetch<T> that throws on 204 (for endpoints that always return a body) and apiFetchOptional<T> that returns T | undefined (for endpoints that may return 204). Alternatively, document the convention that callers should check for undefined when using POST endpoints. The current approach works but adds a small type-safety gap where callers might forget the undefined check.

55e875dc7a36a6fc.reasoning: The flagged duplication between AuthController and ProviderConfigController is limited to standard Spring boilerplate (imports, class-level annotations, logger field, constructor injection). Both controllers have entirely different responsibilities — AuthController handles authentication flows while ProviderConfigController serves provider configuration. The shared boilerplate is idiomatic Spring and extracting it would add unnecessary abstraction.
